title: 911 SAVES Act
plain_summary: The 911 SAVES Act says the government must change how 911 operators are classified in their job system. Right now, these workers are not listed as protective service workers, but they should be because they save lives during emergencies. The bill recognizes that 911 operators do much more than just answer phone calls. The government has 30 days after this bill becomes law to make this change happen.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Supporting Accurate Views of Emergency Services Act of two thousand twenty-five or the nine one one SAVES Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned these important facts. Public Safety Telecommunicators (the people who answer nine one one calls) play a very important role in emergency response. The work these telecommunicators do is much more than just passing messages between the public and first responders like police and firefighters. When children go missing or are kidnapped or hurt, the information these telecommunicators get and the actions they take create the foundation for helping those children. When someone calls nine one one because they are threatening to hurt themselves or others, the telecommunicator is the first person they talk to. The telecommunicator's skills at talking to people in crisis can stop bad situations from getting worse. During shootings at schools or other places, these telecommunicators teach callers how to give first aid and how to stay safe. At the same time, they collect important information to help police officers know what is happening when they arrive. When police officers, firefighters, and Emergency Medical Technicians are in danger and call for help, they call the telecommunicators. These workers often talk to people who are very scared, hurt, or upset. They use their training and experience to recognize important details that could save lives. There have been times when telecommunicators heard the sound of someone loading a shotgun over the phone and warned police officers, which prevented those officers from being hurt or killed. This job has a very serious emotional and physical impact on workers. This impact gets worse because of long work hours and the fact that emergencies happen all day and all night. Research shows that Public Safety Telecommunicators experience trauma that may cause them to develop posttraumatic stress disorder (a mental health condition caused by experiencing or witnessing scary events). Some agencies provide Critical Incident Stress Debriefing teams to help reduce the mental health impact and help both telecommunicators and first responders recover faster after difficult situations. The Standard Occupational Classification system is a way of organizing different types of jobs. It is used only for keeping track of numbers and statistics. Federal agencies use this system to put workers and jobs into categories so they can collect, count, study, and share information about different types of work. Jobs in this system are organized based on what kind of work people do and sometimes based on the skills, education, or training needed to do the work. Putting public safety telecommunicators in the Protective Service Occupations category would fix a mistake in how these jobs are currently classified. It would properly recognize these workers for the life-saving work they do and would make the classification system more accurate and consistent with other similar systems.

sections.title: Section 3 Review of Standard Occupational Classification System
sections.text: The Director of the Office of Management and Budget must, within thirty days after this law is passed, change how public safety telecommunicators are classified. They must put these workers in the protective service occupation category under the Standard Occupational Classification System.
